speaker: interviewer
text: You were two when the war broke out. Do you remember anything about the early part of the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you heard of Nazis and Germans? Anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I was too busy with my-- just playing, playing. I liked to play in the mud. I liked to play. I was just-- I was-- I was very happy. Everybody loved me. I was happy. I was playing. Wherever I went into the family, they all loved me. They always just picked me up, kissed me, hugged me. They always loved me. So I-- I don't remember that time. I only really remember just this in '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were too-- too young to be at school at that stage.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I didn't attend school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your-- were your brothers and sister at school? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: They must have been at school, but I-- I just-- I was just very busy with myself. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things change, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: This changed when my father was taken. This was the first [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] time that I realized that something is happening to me-- to me, to my mother-- I changed. And I felt it with my grandparents also, that something is not so free-flowing anymore. It's tense. People are tense.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you-- from their looks, from what they spoke about? How did you-- as a child, how did you pick that up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I guess they-- they didn't pay so much attention anymore. It was-- I think there was a lot of fear. So I just felt it. I just felt it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I remember the ghetto. The ghetto I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: The first was '42, when your father left for the last time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then things changed in '44, when there were laws made about wearing the yellow star. Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. This was already we were in the ghetto. And we had-- and also in this courtyard.

speaker: interviewer
text: So before you-- the ghetto, had anything happened in the time before you were moved? What do you remember before you went to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened is when my Safar grandparents lived, and my grandfather's brother lived in the same house. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they were young men. Everybody had their sons. And all those sons, they were taken away in 1942. And my grandfather had a brother, Isidor Safar. He was a well-to-do man. He had a store were he was selling ready-made clothes-- mostly men's, but there were some ladies' clothes, too. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] times were getting-- we had to go-- that wasn't right. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] One evening, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he went and he wanted to hide his merchandise from the store. And he went into his backyard. He had a very big house. And in front of the house he had his store. The house was a dark house, I remember. I didn't like that house because it was very dark. It was a nice house, but it was dark-- wasn't too friendly to me. And he went and he dug a-- in the backyard, of his backyard, and he was carrying the merchandise to the-- to this hole. And he covered it. But we didn't know, just afterwards, we knew that, from the tower, they were watching us. There was a tower with a clock, And they were watching us. And then the next morning-- it was a Saturday morning, I think-- they took him, and they put him into jail. And they went to the rest of the Safar houses, to the other brothers, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and they took them into jail.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's they? The Hungarians?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Hungarians. Those were the [INAUDIBLE], yes. This was--

speaker: interviewer
text: There were no Germans in the town at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at this point. I don't-- I haven't seen any.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was Hungarian police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, the [NON-ENGLISH] The gendarme, I think it was. I don't-- there is a word for it, but I don't know in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Arrow Cross?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not the Arrow-- I don't know if it's the Arrow Cross. It has-- it has a proper name. We called it the [NON-ENGLISH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the brothers were taken in there, too. They came to the house, the [NON-ENGLISH] and they had to go. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And a long time is passing, and nobody's showing. Nobody's coming back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there is such a silence, such a silence, I remember. Everything was so silent. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- and the women, the-- the wives and the daughters-- my mother was there-- and they were looking out from the door. The door had a glass door. And I was outside. I was always-- I don't know, always outside. They wanted me to come into the house. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I knew they wanted me to come in, but I didn't listen. I didn't go. And I was just there in the yard. And this was a long walkway toward-- to the-- from the fence, from which-- from the sidewalk or the street. There's the sidewalk, and there was the door to the yard. And it's a long walk up to the Safar house. That's how it seemed to me at that time. As a small child, everything was big and large. And there was a-- a [NON-ENGLISH] came. To me he looked very tall, dressed in boots, uniform, very neat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he had a-- a sword in his hand. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I looked towards the door and they were-- my family said to come, come, and I was very curious what this man is going to do. But somehow, I had a little bit of fear already, because I knew that something is not right here. But I didn't know what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this [NON-ENGLISH]-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I think his name was Churnik-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he took this sword and he started to dig. Slowly he was walking, and he was digging, like this, into the earth, on the sidewalk up to the-- up to the house. Digging, digging. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then he left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I went to the house, and one of the ladies-- I don't know which one, my mother or my aunt-- somebody told me I should go out to the end of the street. They figured, because I'm the youngest, maybe nobody will look at me. I should go out to end of the Jewish street and wait there. Maybe somebody will show up from the uncles. I went a few times. And they told me not to stay too long and come back. And that's what I did a few times. And I came back, and then I went back again, came back, went back. One time when I went back, I was lucky, because my-- this uncle, my mother's uncle, Herman Safar, he came. And I told him--

speaker: interviewer
text: He had been in the jail.

speaker: interviewee
text: He'd been in the jail. They are all in the jail at this time. I said, everybody wants to know what's happening. And they don't leave me alone. They're just sending me out here. So tell me what's happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, don't ask me questions. Just go back and bring a glass of water. I said, yes, but come with me. He says, no, just go and bring me a glass of water. I said, but I have to tell them what's going on, because they don't leave me alone. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he says, just bring me the water. So I went back, and I said, the uncle wants a glass of water. So I was taking the water, and they told me, ask what's happening. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I went back, I gave him the water, and he says, go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, but you have to tell me what's happening. He says, what's happening you don't want to know. I said, but-- because I was a child who was determined--

speaker: interviewer
text: Curious.

speaker: interviewee
text: Curious. Very curious. And he says, well, Isidor-- uncle-- is hung by his beard, and they are beating him. And they have to listen to it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he's taking the water for him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he says, don't ask any more. And I hope he can-- he will survive this day, Uncle Isidore. He says, and don't come back. I said, but they send me. He says, don't come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I went back and I told them what was happening. And I told them not to send me back on the street anymore. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that day, later on, the [NON-ENGLISH] were coming to the Safar house. And they said they want to have those young men, the Safar young men's belongings. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So there was really nothing much left there, and they told them they don't have anything. He says, oh, yes, they all have everything. You always have things. They have. They have. So whatever they have, you just bring it. So they had to bring the tallis. They put it on the table there. That was the middle room in this house. And they had some-- everybody had a suit-- must have been the shabbos suit-- and a pair of shoes. And they put it in the tallis, and they-- and they took it. There wasn't much value there, really, but they took it away. That's what I remember. Did you see your uncles again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were let out in the evening. And this uncle Isidor, who was a handsome man-- tall, very tall man, bearded. He had no children, just a wife. He didn't look the same. He was a broken man. He was just broken-- bent over, broken man. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And the next thing I remember, we are going into the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that in Hajdunanas?

speaker: interviewee
text: It is all in Hajdunanas. We are going into the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So in the same courtyard where the Safar grandparents and the Safar grandfather's brother lived with his family, there was another house, a big house there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there, they put us in one room in a-- in a small-- a little-- like, little room, which was-- the steps were going up to the attic there. So that was fun for me, because on the landing there, I could play. I liked to play. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: But when we moved into the-- this little apartment, my mother sat down-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was in the evening-- and she was cutting out from yellow material stars-- yellow stars. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was up on the bed, and my brother and sister was around my mother. And I was on the bed, so I could see over her head.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was very sad. Her face was sad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I said to mother, when are you going to sew my star? She didn't answer first. So I said to mother, well, when are you going to sew my star? I want my star also. She says, just wait your turn. According to age, I'm making. And she's very, very sad. And then I start to say to my mother, it's not fair. I'm always the last. Everything I get the last. Why do I get the star the last? I thought the star is very-- you know, it's very interesting. So my mother says to me, don't be so anxious. You will get it in time. And I heard it from her voice-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it's-- it's not a happy occasion. So I really was quiet afterwards, and I was waiting for my star. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it took time, because you have to cut out the Star of David, and you had to make it right. She cut it out, she sewed it, and she sewed it on our clothes. And she sewed it on her clothes. So we were marked already, like cattles. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how long we were in the ghetto, but I know it was summer, because it was warm. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then-- [INAUDIBLE] [CRYING] and then, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my grandmother, my grandmother Rosenberg, arrives, very angry. Very angry. And he says-- she says to my mother, here is money. [SOBBING] And I'm up on the step there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was happy first when I saw her coming in, but she didn't pay attention to me. So she-- [CRYING] here is money. Says, go by some food. You see, that was Saturday morning, I think. [SOBBING] And we were told some food should be prepared for the next two weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother says, such a bitter voice, yes, but where shall I get-- where shall I buy anything? My grandmother was so angry, helpless, like somebody locked in a cage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then she left. Money is on the table. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And my mother-- [SOBBING] because there was nothing, really. She made from some fat and flour, she made a paste, actually. It's a-- you know, you cook-- you use this for thickening soup. So she made this, and they had a container like this. And we had some sugar cubes. And we were ordered, that with all of our belongings, we should get it in the yard of the synagogue. And that synagogue was on a huge, huge land. So all the Jews, everybody who was left in Hajdunanas at this time, were in that yard. I still could some-- find some fun. I always just wanted to play. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So there were some children. And I was so happy to see children, because I would play. I will have fun, and it was summer. It was nice. The weather was nice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember there was the fence of the synagogue, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and everybody was in their bun-- with the bundles, you know? Not suitcases, just bundles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And here-- and there was a family, and there was a woman. She was a grown woman, and she was retarded. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to tease her. And she was making funny noises. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And we went there and we called her [? Rella ?], [? Rella, ?] like crazy [? Rella. ?] And then we ran away because of the way she was making that noise. She didn't look-- you know, she looked frightening, and we ran away. But still we want to go back. And my mother noticed that this was me and the other children there. It was a game for us. We didn't know any better. And my mother said, don't do that. You're not supposed to do that. Don't hurt her. [SOBBING] And I heard from her voice again that it's wrong, so I stopped doing it. And here today I'm sorry that I had done that. [CRYING] I didn't know any better. I didn't know that. [CRYING] Next thing, it was such a long time we were there-- so hot. I felt hot. I was tired. [SOBBING] And the next thing I noticed, they have to go on the-- [CRYING] that wagon. The horse was pulling the wagon. A lot of [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] bundles there also. And we were sitting there, a lot of people. But I find again something interesting and fun for me. We were sitting there, back to everybody. There was this little girl. She was really the rabbi's granddaughter. She was younger than me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said to her, oh, isn't it good? We are going to play together. We are going to have such a good games. We are going-- and I was planning, and she says yes, yes, and we are going to play. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I used to play with her before it all started, before the ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were happy about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But somehow, I noticed something is wrong again. It's wrong again, something, because there are young boys, Hungarian boys there. Actually, one I noticed because he was sort of walking. And he had a gun, like on his back, and the gun almost reached the floor, and it was higher than his head, so he must have been a very young boy. He was guarding us. And I knew that I shouldn't be too loud. There has to be something wrong, and I should be really-- be careful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He looked at us. He always looked at us with meanness. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The next thing I noticed, the wagon is moving. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The wagon is moving, and when we go out, when we start to go. You see, they broke down the fences between the properties there, so we went over to the other side of the street. We went on the-- we didn't go out-- come out from the Jewish street, because the ghetto was there. You see, the ghetto was-- it was locked. We were locked in. The beginning of the street, there was a big fence-- wooden fence-- put up. And the end of the street there was a big wooden fence. So we couldn't go. But when they were taking us already to the train, they broke down the fence that was between the property and the other side of the other street. We went out. As the way-- the wagon is moving, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I looked to my right, and I see my [? Safar ?] grandmother, on a summer chair, sitting. She wasn't able to walk already for years. She was put out always, you know, summertime for fresh air, but she had to be carried. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then I said to my mother again, look, grandmother is there. And I feel a terrible pain, a terrible pain in my heart. My grandmother, we are going--

speaker: interviewer
text: And she's left behind.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she's there. She's there, sitting alone, and we are moving. The pain was terrible. My mother says, she will-- she will be with us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then the wagon is going, and we-- [INAUDIBLE] we go through this place, where before, we had to give in all our jewelries. It was given already in-- much before the ghetto, everything-- every Jew had to give in whatever they possessed-- jewelry, money, whatever. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother didn't have too much jewelry, but she had-- and she gave it in, but she had her wedding band. And she wanted to keep this wedding band desperately. This was the only thing from my father. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember there was a, like some sort of a little table, and a chair was stood up-- was standing there, and a man was sitting there. And my mother put her wedding band, put it in her pocket behind her apron. And I saw when she was doing that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she comes to this table. It was her turn, because everything was, you know, in line. You had to stay in line. So to her turn, and my mother says, I don't have any more jewelry. What I had, I gave in, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he says, very rudely-- this was a Hungarian. He says, you just try to get away with everything you can. Everybody-- everybody has a wedding ring, a wedding band. He says, if you're not going to give it, I'm going to search you. And that minute, my mother puts her hand in her pocket, and she takes out the ring, and she puts it on that little table. And I got very angry. I felt angry. So when we passed this little table, and I said to my mother, why did you give it? Why did you give this ring? Why didn't you keep it?

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, I didn't want him to touch me. So she gave. And I understood that. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Next thing I remember that we are in Debrecen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from Hajdunanas--

speaker: interviewee
text: From Hajdunanas, the train took us to Debrecen.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Debrecen. To a ghetto there.

speaker: interviewee
text: To the [INAUDIBLE]. This was a-- we weren't very long there. I don't think so. I don't know. I cannot tell you how long. This [INAUDIBLE] means where they were making bricks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't see the bricks. It was a very empty place, but I remember it was a big place-- the red-- red color it was. And we didn't go up with steps. It was like a-- it was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: A ramp?

speaker: interviewee
text: A ramp, a wide ramp, and that's how we went up there. We are there with our little belongings, and grandmother is with us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But grandmother cannot really move on her own. And we are sitting on the floor. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it seems to me every five minutes we had to get up because an SS is coming in, and then says we had-- as soon as he came, the doorway there-- it wasn't a door. It was opening. It was an opening-- we had to stand up very straight, jump up quickly. Well, we didn't want our grandmother to be seen, because my mother was very afraid that they are going to take her away, because the sick, you had to give out. Anybody who was sick and helpless, they shouldn't [INAUDIBLE]. There. We had to put up and giving her up. And she refused to do that. She refused to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what we did, she told us, every time we had to get up, we have to stay very, very close to each other, so not even a little space should be between us, so grandmother shouldn't be seen. So every time the SS came in, there was some cloth. My mother first threw it on my grandmother, and we stood up and we covered her, like a wall.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was lying in the back. And this happened again and again and again, all the time-- exhausting, frightening. By this time I'm very scared.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this the first time, then, that you'd seen the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Up to this point, it had been Hungarian.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. This is--

speaker: interviewer
text: So in the ghetto in Debrecen--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Debrecen, in the [INAUDIBLE], yes. From here--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll-- we'll-- we'll continue on the next tape--

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. I'll go to the washroom.
